revision.text:
'''能褒野神社'''（のぼのじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[亀山市]]にある神社。[[県社]]。[[日本武尊]]を主神とし、[[建見児王]]および[[弟橘姫命]]を配祀する。
この地は日本武尊が死去した遺跡であると伝えられ、その遺徳をしのぶために、有志が話し合いのうえ神祠の創建を図って、明治17年3月に許可を得て有栖川宮その他の宮家から幣帛料をたまわり、明治28年、賀陽宮家から霊代を拝戴し、鎮座祭がとりおこなわれた。明治42年、建見児王および弟橘姫が合祀された。
日本武尊の墓であるとされる[[能褒野墓]]（[[前方後円墳]]）がある。
{{substub}}

{{神社| 名称=能褒野神社| 所在地=三重県亀山市田村町女ヶ坂1409| 祭神=日本武尊| 社格=県社| 創建=[[明治]]28年（[[1895年]]）| 例祭=[[4月8日]]}}
'''能褒野神社'''（のぼのじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[亀山市]]にある[[神社]]である。[[明治]]時代に創建された神社で、旧[[社格]]は[[県社]]。[[日本武尊]]を主祭神とし、[[建見児王]]および[[弟橘姫命]]を配祀する。
能褒野は日本武尊が死去した地であると伝えられる。一帯には日本武尊の墓に比定される古墳がいくつかあったが、明治12年（[[1879年]]）、内務省が「王塚」あるいは「丁字塚」と呼ばれていた[[前方後円墳]]が日本武尊の墓であると治定し、'''能褒野陵'''とした。能褒野陵は全長約90メートル、高さ約9メートルで、三重県北部最大の前方後円墳である。
その後、地元の有志により日本武尊の遺徳をしのぶための神社の創建が企画された。明治16年に神宮祭主・久邇宮朝彦親王より「能褒野神社」と社号が選定され、明治17年3月に能褒野陵の側に神社創建の許可を得、[[有栖川宮]]その他の[[宮家]]から幣帛料をたまわった。明治28年（[[1895年]]）に社殿が竣工し、神宮祭主・賀陽宮邦憲王より御霊代を拝戴して鎮座祭がとりおこなわれた。
明治42年、元小天宮祭神の弟橘姫と県主神社祭神の建見児王が配祀され、近隣の約40社（式内・那久志里神社、式内・志婆加支神社を含む）を[[神社合祀|合祀]]して村社に列格した。大正14年6月に県社に昇格した。
{{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:のほのしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:府県社]]

{{神社| 名称=能褒野神社| 所在地=三重県亀山市田村町女ヶ坂1409| 祭神=日本武尊| 社格=県社| 創建=[[明治]]28年（[[1895年]]）| 例祭=[[4月8日]]}}

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 能褒野神社 |画像 = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = 三重県亀山市田村町1409 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=能褒野神社}} |祭神 = [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]] |社格 = 旧[[県社]] |創建 = [[明治]]28年（[[1895年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = |例祭 = [[4月8日]] |神事 = |}} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|一の鳥居（[[亀山駅 (三重県)|亀山駅前]]）}}]] '''能褒野神社'''（のぼのじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[亀山市]]田村町にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。 == 祭神 == 祭神は次の3柱。 * 主祭神 ** [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]]（やまとたけるのみこと） * 配祀神 ** [[建貝児王]]（たけかいこのみこ） ** [[弟橘媛|弟橘姫命]]（おとたちばなひめのみこと） ==歴史== 『[[古事記]]』や『[[日本書紀]]』によると、日本武尊は能褒野（能煩野）で死去したという。墓は『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]にも「能襃野墓」と見えるが、後世に所在不明となった。一帯には日本武尊の陵墓と伝えられる古墳がいくつかあったが、[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）に「王塚」あるいは「丁字塚」と呼ばれていた[[前方後円墳]]（現在の[[能褒野王塚古墳]]）が、内務省によって「'''能褒野墓'''」に治定された。 その後、地元の有志により日本武尊の遺徳をしのぶため能褒野陵周辺での神社の創建が企画されたのが、当社の淵源になる。明治16年（[[1883年]]）に[[伊勢神宮|神宮]][[祭主]]・[[久邇宮朝彦親王]]より「能褒野神社」と社号が選定され、明治17年（[[1884年]]）3月に能褒野陵の側に神社創建の許可を得、[[有栖川宮]]その他の[[宮家]]から幣帛料をたまわった。明治28年（[[1895年]]）に社殿が竣工され、神宮祭主・賀陽宮邦憲王より御霊代を拝戴して鎮座祭がとりおこなわれた。 明治42年（[[1909年]]）には元小天宮祭神の弟橘姫と県主神社祭神の建貝児王が配祀され、さらに近隣の約40社（式内・那久志里神社、式内・志婆加支神社を含む）が[[神社合祀|合祀]]されたうえで、[[近代社格制度]]において[[村社]]に列した。その後、[[大正]]14年（[[1925年]]）6月に[[県社]]に昇格した。 == 交通アクセス == * 鉄道：[[東海旅客鉄道]]（JR東海）[[関西本線]] [[井田川駅]] （徒歩約30分） {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:のほのしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:亀山市の歴史]] [[Category:亀山市の建築物]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説]]

{{神社 |名称 = 能褒野神社 |画像 = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]

{{神社 |名称 = 能褒野神社 |画像 = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
鳥居と拝殿 |所在地 = 三重県亀山市田村町1409 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=能褒野神社}} |祭神 = [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]] |社格 = 旧[[県社]] |創建 = [[明治]]28年（[[1895年]]） |本殿 = |別名 = |例祭 = [[4月8日]] |神事 = |}} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|一の鳥居（[[亀山駅 (三重県)|亀山駅前]]）}}]] '''能褒野神社'''（のぼのじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[亀山市]]田村町にある[[神社]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[県社]]。 == 祭神 == 祭神は次の3柱。 * 主祭神 ** [[ヤマトタケル|日本武尊]]（やまとたけるのみこと） * 配祀神 ** [[建貝児王]]（たけかいこのみこ） ** [[弟橘媛|弟橘姫命]]（おとたちばなひめのみこと） ==歴史== 『[[古事記]]』や『[[日本書紀]]』によると、日本武尊は能褒野（能煩野）で死去したという。墓は『[[延喜式]]』[[諸陵寮]]にも「能襃野墓」と見えるが、後世に所在不明となった。一帯には日本武尊の陵墓と伝えられる古墳がいくつかあったが、[[明治]]12年（[[1879年]]）に「王塚」あるいは「丁字塚」と呼ばれていた[[前方後円墳]]（現在の[[能褒野王塚古墳]]）が、内務省によって「'''能褒野墓'''」に治定された。 その後、地元の有志により日本武尊の遺徳をしのぶため能褒野陵周辺での神社の創建が企画されたのが、当社の淵源になる。明治16年（[[1883年]]）に[[伊勢神宮|神宮]][[祭主]]・[[久邇宮朝彦親王]]より「能褒野神社」と社号が選定され、明治17年（[[1884年]]）3月に能褒野陵の側に神社創建の許可を得、[[有栖川宮]]その他の[[宮家]]から幣帛料をたまわった。明治28年（[[1895年]]）に社殿が竣工され、神宮祭主・賀陽宮邦憲王より御霊代を拝戴して鎮座祭がとりおこなわれた。 明治42年（[[1909年]]）には元小天宮祭神の弟橘姫と県主神社祭神の建貝児王が配祀され、さらに近隣の約40社（式内・那久志里神社、式内・志婆加支神社を含む）が[[神社合祀|合祀]]されたうえで、[[近代社格制度]]において[[村社]]に列した。その後、[[大正]]14年（[[1925年]]）6月に[[県社]]に昇格した。 == 交通アクセス == * 鉄道：[[井田川駅]]（[[関西線 (名古屋地区)|関西線 (JR東海)]]）徒歩約30分 {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:のほのしんしや}} [[Category:三重県の神社]] [[Category:亀山市の歴史]] [[Category:亀山市の建築物]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:ヤマトタケル伝説]] [[Category:弟橘媛]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |Image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |Location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: * {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) * {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |Image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]

The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[category:new pages]][[ja:能褒野神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |Image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |Location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: * {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) * {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[ja:能褒野神社]]

The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:new pages]]能褒野神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |Image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |Location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: * {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) * {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] 能褒野神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |Image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |Location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: * {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: * {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) * {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Pages where the interlanguage links were botched]][[Category:new pages]][[ja:[[Category:Pages where the It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ** {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ** {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ** {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. {{Shrine |Name = Nobono Shrine |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |Coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}} '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}} '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nobono Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nobono Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nobono Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nobono Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Nobono Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, approved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:refreshing the redirects]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, aqqroved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, aqqroved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[Category:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Nobono Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=draft}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=draft}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=draft}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}} |Shrine rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|12=draft}} under the {{ill|modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=draft}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=draft}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=draft}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|12=draft}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|12=draft}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=draft}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|12=draft}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=draft}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=draft}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=draft}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|12=draft}}. The shrine was initially ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=draft}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社}} under the {{ill|modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft}}. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|qq=draft}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|qq=draft}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式|qq=draft}} {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|ja|久邇宮朝彦親王|qq=draft}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|ja|神社合祀|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy|qq=draft}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|ja|井田川駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Nobono Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|qq=}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryoryo|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]]

His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryoryo|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:亀山市の建築物]] [[Category:亀山市の歴史]]

His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryoryo|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:三重県の旧県社]] [[Category:Buildings in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:弟橘媛]] {{Commons category|Nobono-jinja}}
