{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryoryo|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:弟橘媛]] {{Commons category|Nobono-jinja}}

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]

In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11612066]] Wikidata name: Nobono Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG|280px]]
Torii and worship hall |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34|53|9.01|N|136|29|0.60|E|region:JP-24_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Nobono Shrine}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = }} [[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[:en:Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|lt=Ken-sha|1=Modern_system_of_ranked_Shinto_shrines#Metropolitan_and_Prefectural_Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと) == History == According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryoryo|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryoryo|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi." Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14). == Access == * By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. {{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}} {{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] }} [[sn:Nobono Shrine]] [[ja:能褒野神社]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings in Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:History of Kameyama, Mie]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] {{Commons category|Nobono-jinja}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nobono-jinja]][[ja:能褒野神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11612066]] Wikidata name: Nobono Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34.886111|N|136.482778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = |location = {{ill|Kameyama|qid=Q646858}} |deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258}} }}
[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[:en:Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|qid=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== History ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."

In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|qq=|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nobono-jinja]][[ja:能褒野神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612066]] Wikidata name: Nobono Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34.886111|N|136.482778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = |location = {{ill|Kameyama|WD=Q646858}} |deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} }}
[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[:en:Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
{{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}}
{{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Nobono-jinja]][[ja:能褒野神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Nobono-jinja]][[ja:能褒野神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612066]] Wikidata name: Nobono Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34.886111|N|136.482778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = |location = {{ill|Kameyama|WD=Q646858}} |deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} }}
[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of [[:en:Kameyama Station (Mie)|Kameyama Station]])}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]].
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the [[:en:Engishiki|Engishiki]] {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Prince Kuni Asahiko|Prince Arisugawa Taruhito]], a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the [[:en:Shrine Consolidation Policy|Shrine Consolidation Policy]]. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from [[:en:Idagawa Station|Idagawa Station]] on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of {{ill|Kameyama Station (Mie)|en|Kameyama Station (Mie)|WD=Q5358681|lt=Kameyama Station}})}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34.886111|N|136.482778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = |location = {{ill|Kameyama|WD=Q646858}} |deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} }}
[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of {{ill|Kameyama Station (Mie)|en|Kameyama Station (Mie)|WD=Q5358681|lt=Kameyama Station}})}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
{{Commonscat|Nobono-jinja}}
{{translated page|ja|能褒野神社|version=87700717|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:能褒野神社]]

[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of {{ill|Kameyama Station (Mie)|en|Kameyama Station (Mie)|WD=Q5358681|lt=Kameyama Station}})}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Prefectural Shrine|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)

Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station|1=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of {{ill|Kameyama Station (Mie)|en|Kameyama Station (Mie)|WD=Q5358681|lt=Kameyama Station|1=Kameyama Station (Mie)}})}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|1=Prince Kuni Asahiko}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).

* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station|1=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Nobono Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Nobono-jinja torii.JPG |location = Tamura Town 1409, Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture |coordinates = {{Coord|34.886111|N|136.482778|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deities = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} |Shrine Rank = Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} |Founding = [[1895]] (Meiji 28) |Main hall = |Alternative name = |Festival = [[April 8]] |Rites = |location = {{ill|Kameyama|WD=Q646858|1=Kameyama, Mie}} |deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}} }}
[[File:Kameyama Station (Mie), torii.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{center|First Torii (in front of {{ill|Kameyama Station (Mie)|en|Kameyama Station (Mie)|WD=Q5358681|lt=Kameyama Station|1=Kameyama Station (Mie)}})}}]] '''Nobono Shrine''' (能褒野神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Kameyama City, Mie Prefecture. It is classified as a former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|県社|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}{{efn|Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}.
== Enshrined Deities ==
The shrine enshrines the following three deities: * Main deity: ''' {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}} (やまとたけるのみこと) * Associated deities: ''' {{ill|Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja|建貝児王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takekaiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (たけかいこのみこ) ''' {{ill|Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Tachibanahime-no-Mikoto|lt=Ototachibana-hime|ja|弟橘媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ototachibana-hime|qq=}} (おとたちばなひめのみこと)
== History ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Yamato Takeru passed away at Nobono (能煩野). His tomb is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|WD=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|Shoryō-ryō|ja|諸陵寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shoryō-ryō|qq=}} as "Nobono no Misasagi" (能襃野墓), but its exact location became unclear over time. Several ancient tombs in the area were traditionally associated with Yamato Takeru, but in 1879 (Meiji 12), the "Ōtsuka" or "T-shaped mound" (now known as the {{ill|Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|能褒野王塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobono Ōtsuka Kofun|qq=}}), a keyhole-shaped kofun, was designated by the Ministry of Home Affairs as "Nobono no Misasagi."
Subsequently, local residents proposed establishing a shrine near the Nobono Tomb to honor Yamato Takeru's legacy. In 1883 (Meiji 16), the {{ill|Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮祭主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chief Priest of Ise Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Prince Kuni Asahiko|en|Prince Kuni Asahiko|WD=Q5370435|lt=Prince Arisugawa Taruhito|1=Prince Kuni Asahiko}}, a11roved the name "Nobono Shrine." Permission to construct the shrine near the Nobono Tomb was granted in March 1884 (Meiji 17), and contributions were received from the Imperial family, including the House of Arisugawa. The shrine’s main hall was completed in 1895 (Meiji 28), and the sacred spirit (goshintai) was enshrined in a ceremony led by Prince Kuni Kuninori, the Chief Priest of Ise Shrine.
In 1909 (Meiji 42), two deities—Ototachibana-hime from the former {{ill|Kotengu Shrine|ja|小天宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotengu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Takekaiko no Mikoto from the former {{ill|Kenushu Shrine|ja|県主神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenushu Shrine|1=Agatanushi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11582625}}—were added to the shrine. Around 40 nearby shrines, including the {{ill|Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja|那久志里神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Nakushiri Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja|志婆加支神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha Shibakaki Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were merged into Nobono Shrine under the {{ill|Shrine Consolidation Policy|en|Shrine Consolidation Policy|WD=Q11590700|lt=Shrine Consolidation Policy|1=Shrine Consolidation Policy}}. The shrine was initially Ranked as a village shrine but was promoted to a prefectural shrine in 1925 (Taishō 14).
== Access ==
* By train: About 30 minutes on foot from {{ill|Idagawa Station|en|Idagawa Station|WD=Q5358568|lt=Idagawa Station|1=Idagawa Station}} on the {{ill|Kansai Main Line|ja|関西本線 (名古屋地区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai Main Line|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
